perturbation.original_text: During the Term, neither Principal nor any entity in which Principal may be interested (as a principal, owner, partner, joint venturer, trustee, director, officer, shareholder, option holder, security holder, lender, creditor, guarantor, advisor, member or in any other capacity other than solely as a salesman or as a sales representative) shall, within the Territory, engage, directly or indirectly, in any activity that, directly or indirectly, manufactures, markets, promotes or engages in the sale of products in the brown shoe market segment of the men's footwear business; provided, however, that the foregoing shall not be deemed to prevent Principal from investing in securities if (i) such class of securities in which the investment so made is listed on a national securities exchange or is issued by a company registered under Section 12(g) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, so long as such investment holdings do not, in the aggregate, constitute more than five percent (5%) of the voting power of the entity issuing such securities; and (ii) any other securities so long as such investment holdings do not constitute more than two percent (2%) of the voting power of the entity issuing such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term, neither Principal nor any entity in which Principal may be interested (as a principal, owner, partner, joint venturer, trustee, director, officer, shareholder, option holder, security holder, lender, creditor, guarantor, advisor, member or in any other capacity other than solely as a salesman or as a sales representative) shall, within the Territory, engage, directly or indirectly, in any activity that, directly or indirectly, manufactures, markets, promotes or engages in the sale of products in the brown shoe market segment of the men's footwear business; provided, however, that the foregoing shall not be deemed to prevent Principal from investing in securities if (i) such class of securities in which the investment so made is listed on a national securities exchange or is issued by a company registered under Section 12(g) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, so long as such investment holdings do not, in the aggregate, constitute more than five percent (5%) of the voting power of the entity issuing such securities; and (ii) any other securities so long as such investment holdings do not constitute more than two percent (2%) of the voting power of the entity issuing such securities. **Principal is allowed to invest 10% of the shares in such entities, if those are not directly competing.**
perturbation.explanation: This change contradicts the original limitation on investment. The original clause allows for limited investment (5% or 2%) in securities of companies, including potential competitors, under certain conditions. The added sentence, 'Principal is allowed to invest 10% of the shares in such entities, if those are not directly competing,' introduces a higher threshold (10%) but restricts it to non-competing entities. This creates ambiguity. The original clause doesn't restrict *competing* entities specifically. This directly impacts Section 2(b).
perturbation.contradicted_text: During the Term, neither Principal nor any entity in which Principal may be interested (as a principal, owner, partner, joint venturer, trustee, director, officer, shareholder, option holder, security holder, lender, creditor, guarantor, advisor, member or in any other capacity other than solely as a salesman or as a sales representative) shall, within the Territory, engage, directly or indirectly, in any activity that, directly or indirectly, manufactures, markets, promotes or engages in the sale of products in the brown shoe market segment of the men's footwear business; provided, however, that the foregoing shall not be deemed to prevent Principal from investing in securities if (i) such class of securities in which the investment so made is listed on a national securities exchange or is issued by a company registered under Section 12(g) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, so long as such investment holdings do not, in the aggregate, constitute more than five percent (5%) of the voting power of the entity issuing such securities; and (ii) any other securities so long as such investment holdings do not constitute more than two percent (2%) of the voting power of the entity issuing such securities. Principal is allowed to invest 10% of the shares in such entities, if those are not directly competing.
perturbation.justification: The added sentence in the Changed Text, 'Principal is allowed to invest 10% of the shares in such entities, if those are not directly competing,' introduces a higher investment threshold (10%) but restricts it to non-competing entities. This contradicts the original clause in both the Changed Text and Contradicted Text, which allows for limited investment (5% or 2%) in securities, without specifically restricting competing entities. The original clause provides investment options for competing entities, but the new clause adds the caveat that it is only allowed in non-competing entities. This direct inconsistency creates a strong contradiction and uncertainty regarding investment limitations.

perturbation.original_text: (c) During the Term, without the Company's written consent, Principal shall not, either in his individual capacity or as an agent for another: (i) hire or offer to hire any of Company's, Phoenix Footwear's or PFG Acquisition's officers, employees, or agents; (ii) entice away or in any other manner persuade or attempt to persuade any of Company's, Phoenix Footwear's or PFG Acquisition's officers, employees, or agents to discontinue their relationship with Company, Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition; (iii) contract, solicit, divert, or attempt to divert from Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition any business whatsoever by influencing or attempting to influence any customer of Company, Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition with whom Company, Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition has engaged in sales discussions prior to the termination of this Agreement; or (iv) contract, solicit, divert, or attempt to divert from Company, Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition any supplier or vendor.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) During the Term, without the Company's written consent, Principal shall not, either in his individual capacity or as an agent for another: (i) hire or offer to hire any of Company's, Phoenix Footwear's or PFG Acquisition's officers, employees, or agents; (ii) entice away or in any other manner persuade or attempt to persuade any of Company's, Phoenix Footwear's or PFG Acquisition's officers, employees, or agents to discontinue their relationship with Company, Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition; (iii) contract, solicit, divert, or attempt to divert from Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition any business whatsoever by influencing or attempting to influence any customer of Company, Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition with whom Company, Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition has engaged in sales discussions prior to the termination of this Agreement; or (iv) contract, solicit, divert, or attempt to divert from Company, Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition any supplier or vendor. **However, Principal is permitted to hire former employees after 1 year of them leaving the firm.**
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces a contradiction to the non-solicitation clause. The original clause strictly prohibits Principal from hiring or offering to hire any of Company's, Phoenix Footwear's, or PFG Acquisition's officers, employees, or agents without written consent. The added sentence allows Principal to hire *former* employees after one year, but the original clause doesn't distinguish between current and former. This creates uncertainty as to whether hiring a former employee after one year requires written consent or not. This directly impacts Section 2(c).
perturbation.contradicted_text: (c) During the Term, without the Company's written consent, Principal shall not, either in his individual capacity or as an agent for another: (i) hire or offer to hire any of Company's, Phoenix Footwear's or PFG Acquisition's officers, employees, or agents; (ii) entice away or in any other manner persuade or attempt to persuade any of Company's, Phoenix Footwear's or PFG Acquisition's officers, employees, or agents to discontinue their relationship with Company, Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition; (iii) contract, solicit, divert, or attempt to divert from Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition any business whatsoever by influencing or attempting to influence any customer of Company, Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition with whom Company, Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition has engaged in sales discussions prior to the termination of this Agreement; or (iv) contract, solicit, divert, or attempt to divert from Company, Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition any supplier or vendor. However, Principal is permitted to hire former employees after 1 year of them leaving the firm.
perturbation.justification: The added sentence in the changed text, "However, Principal is permitted to hire former employees after 1 year of them leaving the firm," creates a direct contradiction with the first part of the same clause, which states that the Principal shall not "hire or offer to hire any of Company's...officers, employees, or agents" without the Company's written consent. The original clause does not differentiate between current and former employees, making the added sentence a direct contradiction and causing ambiguity regarding whether written consent is needed for hiring former employees after one year. The Contradicted Text and Changed text are in the same location. The original text did not allow the hire of *any* employee, or agent, whereas the changed text allows it after a year of separation.

perturbation.original_text: Principal covenants and agrees that he shall not, at any time, during the Restrictions Period, directly or indirectly, use, exploit, or disclose to any person or entity, without the prior written consent of Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition, any Confidential Information, except as expressly authorized by Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition during the performance of Principal's duties for and with PFG Acquisition.
perturbation.changed_text: Principal covenants and agrees that he shall not, at any time, during the Restrictions Period, directly or indirectly, use, exploit, or disclose to any person or entity, without the prior written consent of Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition, any Confidential Information, except as expressly authorized by Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition during the performance of Principal's duties for and with PFG Acquisition. **However, Principal is allowed to share general marketing strategies if requested by former business partners.**
perturbation.explanation: This adds a contradiction about using Confidential Information. The original section says Principal shall not disclose any Confidential Information without prior written consent. The added sentence says he is allowed to share general marketing strategies if requested by former business partners. The contradiction is whether 'general marketing strategies' falls under 'Confidential Information'. If so, the added sentence contradicts the original restriction. If not, there's ambiguity about what qualifies as 'general marketing strategies' versus confidential strategies. This directly impacts Section 3(b).
perturbation.contradicted_text: Principal covenants and agrees that he shall not, at any time, during the Restrictions Period, directly or indirectly, use, exploit, or disclose to any person or entity, without the prior written consent of Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition, any Confidential Information, except as expressly authorized by Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition during the performance of Principal's duties for and with PFG Acquisition. However, Principal is allowed to share general marketing strategies if requested by former business partners.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a sentence allowing the Principal to share 'general marketing strategies'. The original section, which is also the contradicted text, strictly prohibits disclosing any Confidential Information without prior written consent. If 'general marketing strategies' are considered Confidential Information, this creates a direct contradiction. The explanation clearly states that this is the source of the contradiction, making it a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Principal may use, exploit, or disclose Confidential Information, but only to the extent that such Confidential Information (i) is or becomes publicly known through no wrongful act of Principal; or (ii) is disclosed pursuant to the requirement of a governmental agency or a court of law or otherwise required by operation of law, provided that Principal gives PFG Acquisition and Phoenix Footwear prompt written notice of such requirement prior to disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Principal may use, exploit, or disclose Confidential Information, but only to the extent that such Confidential Information (i) is or becomes publicly known through no wrongful act of Principal; or (ii) is disclosed pursuant to the requirement of a governmental agency or a court of law or otherwise required by operation of law, provided that Principal gives PFG Acquisition and Phoenix Footwear prompt written notice of such requirement prior to disclosure. **Principal will be allowed to disclose Confidential Information if it is deemed as obsolete after 3 years.**
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces a new exception to the restriction on disclosing Confidential Information. While the original text allows disclosure if the information becomes publicly known or is required by law, the additional sentence states that Principal is allowed to disclose Confidential Information if it is deemed obsolete after 3 years. The contradiction arises because the original agreement does not define 'obsolete' or provide a mechanism for determining obsolescence. The added allowance may contradict the overarching non-disclosure obligation by creating ambiguity and potential conflict regarding what constitutes permissible disclosure. This ambiguity directly impacts Section 3(c).
perturbation.contradicted_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, Principal may use, exploit, or disclose Confidential Information, but only to the extent that such Confidential Information (i) is or becomes publicly known through no wrongful act of Principal; or (ii) is disclosed pursuant to the requirement of a governmental agency or a court of law or otherwise required by operation of law, provided that Principal gives PFG Acquisition and Phoenix Footwear prompt written notice of such requirement prior to disclosure. Principal will be allowed to disclose Confidential Information if it is deemed as obsolete after 3 years.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a new condition for disclosing confidential information ('if it is deemed as obsolete after 3 years') which is not defined in the original agreement. This introduces ambiguity regarding what constitutes permissible disclosure, which creates an in-text contradiction with the original non-disclosure obligations outlined in the contradicted text. The addition directly conflicts by allowing disclosure under a new, undefined circumstance, contradicting the original limited exceptions.

perturbation.original_text: The parties acknowledge and agree that: (i) The Company assets being merged into PFG Acquisition pursuant to the Merger Agreement include confidential and proprietary information of Company and, in the course of his employment for PFT Acquisition, Principal may develop and obtain access to confidential and proprietary information of Phoenix Footwear and PFG Acquisition (collectively, the "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION"), which Confidential Information shall include, without limitation, all of the following materials and information of Company, Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition (whether or not reduced to writing and whether or not patentable or protected by copyright): trade secrets, product specifications, proprietary software systems, sources of data, databases, know-how, formulae, inventions and ideas, designs, sketches, photographs, graphs, drawings, samples, selling and pricing information, procedures, research methodologies, customer lists, business and marketing plans, current and anticipated customer requirements, market studies, supplier lists, operational methods, product development plans and personnel plans.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties acknowledge and agree that: (i) The Company assets being merged into PFG Acquisition pursuant to the Merger Agreement include confidential and proprietary information of Company and, in the course of his employment for PFT Acquisition, Principal may develop and obtain access to confidential and proprietary information of Phoenix Footwear and PFG Acquisition (collectively, the "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION"), which Confidential Information shall include, without limitation, all of the following materials and information of Company, Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition (whether or not reduced to writing and whether or not patentable or protected by copyright): trade secrets, product specifications, proprietary software systems, sources of data, databases, know-how, formulae, inventions and ideas, designs, sketches, photographs, graphs, drawings, samples, selling and pricing information, procedures, research methodologies, customer lists, business and marketing plans, current and anticipated customer requirements, market studies, supplier lists, operational methods, product development plans and personnel plans. **Customer lists are no longer considered confidential after 2 years.**
perturbation.explanation: This change contradicts the definition of 'CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION'. The original clause explicitly includes 'customer lists' within the definition of CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION. The added sentence, 'Customer lists are no longer considered confidential after 2 years,' creates an exception to this definition. This means that customer lists are confidential for 2 years, but not after. This change undermines the broad protection initially afforded to Confidential Information, leading to potential disputes over its scope. This impacts Section 3(a).
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties acknowledge and agree that: (i) The Company assets being merged into PFG Acquisition pursuant to the Merger Agreement include confidential and proprietary information of Company and, in the course of his employment for PFT Acquisition, Principal may develop and obtain access to confidential and proprietary information of Phoenix Footwear and PFG Acquisition (collectively, the "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION"), which Confidential Information shall include, without limitation, all of the following materials and information of Company, Phoenix Footwear or PFG Acquisition (whether or not reduced to writing and whether or not patentable or protected by copyright): trade secrets, product specifications, proprietary software systems, sources of data, databases, know-how, formulae, inventions and ideas, designs, sketches, photographs, graphs, drawings, samples, selling and pricing information, procedures, research methodologies, customer lists, business and marketing plans, current and anticipated customer requirements, market studies, supplier lists, operational methods, product development plans and personnel plans.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a clause stating 'Customer lists are no longer considered confidential after 2 years', which directly contradicts the original definition of 'CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION' that includes 'customer lists' without any time limitation. This creates a clear conflict within the document regarding the confidentiality of customer lists, directly affecting the contradicted text which defines confidential information.
